skill.name: task-execution-engine
skill.description: Executes implementation tasks from markdown design documents by parsing checkboxes, selecting the next available task, and updating task status.
skill.summary: Run and resume markdown-based implementation task lists.
skill.author: notedit

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography alerts were reviewed and are false positives caused by markdown examples, inline backticks, and documentation text. The skill does include a Python script that reads and rewrites user-supplied markdown files, and its instructions encourage autonomous implementation loops, so publication should include a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Markdown File Is Rewritten
medium_findings.description: The task manager accepts a file path from the command line, reads that file, and writes updated task status back to the same path. This is legitimate for a task manager, but it can alter the wrong file if the caller supplies an unintended path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The read and write operations are direct and tied to the caller-provided --file argument. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is controlled file mutation rather than malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: Autonomous Implementation Loop Requires User Oversight
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to continue implementation tasks without stopping for clarification and to make autonomous decisions from codebase patterns. This can cause unintended code changes when task requirements are incomplete or ambiguous.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The unattended execution instructions are explicit in the skill text. The risk depends on how the user invokes the skill, so medium severity is appropriate.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings. Reviewed locations are markdown fences, inline formatting, task examples, or Python regular expressions that parse backtick-delimited attributes, with no subprocess or shell execution evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are documentation formatting or regex parsing, not execution APIs. No evidence found of os.system, subprocess, eval, exec, or network-backed command execution in the inspected files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts in Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators, but the reviewed locations describe markdown files, task examples, or hypothetical authentication work. No cryptographic implementation or weak hash algorithm usage was found in the audited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged files do not implement hashing, encryption, or token signing. References to authentication-related tasks are examples for user projects, not executable crypto code in the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Alert
low_findings.description: The reported system reconnaissance finding points to a task example in the skill documentation. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment collection, process listing, or network discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are markdown examples for implementation tasks. The inspected script only parses and updates task lists, with no reconnaissance APIs or commands present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes to Caller-Selected Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The script writes updated markdown content back to the path supplied through the command line. This is expected behavior, but it should be limited to trusted design documents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write calls are visible and deterministic. The risk is bounded because the script does not execute file contents or contact external services.

content.value_statement: Implementation plans often stall when task state lives outside the design document. This skill parses markdown checkboxes, selects the next task, and records completion or failure in place.
actual_capabilities: Parse implementation tasks from a markdown section named Implementation Tasks.
Select the next pending task based on priority and completed dependencies.
Mark tasks as completed, failed, or pending in the source document.
Update acceptance criteria checkboxes when a task is completed.
Print task status summaries and list parsed tasks.
Support JSON output for automation around task selection and status checks.
limitations: It only understands the documented markdown checkbox format.
It does not implement application features by itself.
It can rewrite the wrong file if given the wrong path.
It does not validate whether completed code meets product requirements.

use_cases.title: Resume Interrupted Feature Work
use_cases.description: Continue from the first pending task in a design document after an interrupted coding session.

use_cases.title: Run Structured Implementation Plans
use_cases.description: Turn a design document into an ordered execution loop with visible status updates.

use_cases.title: Track AI Coding Progress
use_cases.description: Keep task state inside the same markdown document used to guide an AI coding assistant.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Task List
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin implementation from a prepared design document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Start implementation from docs/designs/example.md and work through the next available task.

prompt_templates.title: Resume Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Continue after previous tasks were completed or failed.
prompt_templates.prompt: Resume the implementation tasks in docs/designs/example.md and report the next task before editing files.

prompt_templates.title: Check Progress First
prompt_templates.scenario: Review task state before making changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show the current task status for docs/designs/example.md, then identify the next unblocked task.

prompt_templates.title: Execute With Failure Tracking
prompt_templates.scenario: Run tasks while preserving reasons for blocked or failed work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Execute the remaining tasks in docs/designs/example.md. Mark any blocked task as failed with a short reason and continue.

output_examples.input: A design document contains three implementation tasks. The first task has the highest priority and no dependencies.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies the first task, lists its target files and acceptance criteria, then starts implementation.

output_examples.input: A task is completed after its files are updated and acceptance criteria are verified.
output_examples.output: The skill marks the task and its criteria as completed in the markdown document and moves to the next available task.

output_examples.input: A task cannot be completed because a required package is missing.
output_examples.output: The skill marks the task as failed, records the reason, and continues with any unblocked remaining task.

best_practices: Review the design document before starting unattended execution.
Keep each task small enough to verify with focused tests.
Use clear dependency names that exactly match task titles.
anti_patterns: Do not point the task manager at files that are not task documents.
Do not use vague acceptance criteria that cannot be verified.
Do not run unattended mode on high-risk production changes without review.

faq.question: Does this skill create implementation plans?
faq.answer: No. It executes task lists that already exist in a markdown design document.

faq.question: Can it resume work after an interruption?
faq.answer: Yes. It reads the markdown checkboxes and selects the first pending task with satisfied dependencies.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands from the task file?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it executes task file contents. The included script parses and updates markdown task status.

faq.question: What file format does it require?
faq.answer: It expects a markdown section named Implementation Tasks with checkbox task lines and optional attributes.

faq.question: Can it damage files?
faq.answer: It can rewrite the path supplied to the script, so users should provide only trusted markdown design files.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning for local file writes and autonomous implementation behavior.
